title: Build a 72-Hour Emergency Kit
summary: Build a centralized 72-hour emergency kit with water, food, lighting, a radio, first aid supplies, medications, sanitation items, documents, and communication tools so your household can shelter in place or grab and go.
quickAnswer: Store at least 3 days of water (1 gallon per person per day), shelf-stable food, a flashlight, batteries, a radio, first aid supplies, prescription medications, sanitation items, copies of key documents, and a whistle in one clearly labeled kit.
whenToUse: Use before storms, earthquakes, floods, wildfires, or prolonged service disruptions when you may need to remain at home or evacuate without outside help.
Use when building or refreshing household emergency supplies.
preferredAction: Build one centralized kit sized for everyone in the household for at least 72 hours.
backupAction: If you cannot fully stock 72 hours, start with water, a flashlight, a radio, medications, and basic food, then expand over time.
stepByStepActions: Choose a durable container and a storage location that is easy to access in an emergency.
Store at least 1 gallon of water per person per day for 3 days.
Pack 3 days of shelf-stable, easy-to-eat food and a manual can opener.
Add a flashlight, spare batteries, and a battery-powered or hand-crank radio.
Include a first aid kit, prescription medications, and essential medical supplies.
Pack sanitation items, hygiene supplies, blankets, and sturdy shoes.
Keep copies of important documents, emergency contacts, and cash in the kit.
Add a whistle, local maps, a phone charger or power bank, and basic tools such as a multi-tool.
Include infant, child, pet, or special-needs items as required.
Review and refresh the kit every 6 to 12 months, replacing expired items.
warnings: Utilities, tap water, and fuel may be unavailable after major disasters.
Temperature-sensitive medicines and devices need special planning during outages.
Kits not maintained or rotated regularly may contain expired food, medications, or depleted batteries when needed.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on foods that require large amounts of water or fuel to prepare.
Do not scatter supplies randomly around the house without one clearly marked core kit.
redFlags: Running out of life-sustaining medication, safe water, or basic shelter supplies during an active emergency needs urgent outside help or official support.
Chest pain, confusion, breathing trouble, or medication failure should be treated as urgent medical issues.
You are forced to evacuate immediately with no kit available.
Kit contents are expired or unusable when an emergency strikes.
preparednessTips: Aim toward a 2-week home supply over time, even if 72 hours is your baseline.
Keep a written inventory taped to or stored inside the kit.
Consider keeping a smaller secondary kit in your car or workplace.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core kit item list and practical household baseline.

notes: Consolidated from build-72-hour-kit (canonical) and 72-hour-home-survival-kit-shelter-at-home (archived). Steps merged: 10 total (7 from archive expanded, 3 unique additions from canonical including whistle, maps, tools). Layer upgraded from scenario to preparedness. Source refs from canonical (4 real URLs). Archive slug should be set to review_status:archived after this version is approved.

tools.name: Flashlight
tools.description: A battery-powered portable light source for illumination in low-light conditions
tools.context: Essential for visibility during power outages or nighttime emergencies

tools.name: Batteries
tools.description: Portable electrochemical power sources for flashlights, radios, and electronic devices
tools.context: Powers flashlight, radio, and other critical electronics in the kit

tools.name: Radio
tools.description: A radio used in survival and emergency preparedness
tools.context: Receives weather alerts and emergency broadcasts when cell networks are down

tools.name: First Aid Kit
tools.description: A first aid kit used in survival and emergency preparedness
tools.context: Provides wound care, bandages, and basic medical supplies for injuries

tools.name: Whistle
tools.description: A small device that produces a loud high-pitched sound for signaling and attracting attention
tools.context: Signals for help when voice cannot carry far enough

tools.name: Multi-tool
tools.description: A compact folding tool combining pliers, blades, screwdrivers, and other implements
tools.context: Provides cutting, prying, and repair capability in a single compact tool

tools.name: Map
tools.description: A printed representation of terrain, trails, and landmarks used for navigation
tools.context: Enables route planning when GPS and cell service are unavailable

tools.name: Power bank
tools.description: A rechargeable battery pack used to charge phones and small electronic devices
tools.context: Keeps phone charged for emergency calls and information access

tools.name: Water bottle
tools.description: A sealed container for carrying and storing drinking water
tools.context: Stores portion of the 1-gallon-per-person-per-day water supply

tools.name: Emergency blanket
tools.description: A lightweight reflective sheet that retains body heat and provides weather protection
tools.context: Provides warmth and weather protection if heating is unavailable

tools.name: Garbage bags
tools.description: Large heavy-duty plastic bags useful for rain protection, ground cover, and waste
tools.context: Used for sanitation, waste containment, and improvised waterproofing

tools.name: Permanent marker
tools.description: A waterproof writing instrument used for labeling gear and leaving messages
tools.context: Labels containers, marks dates on stored items, and leaves messages if evacuating
